source.title: Crane Boom Structural Damage - Main Pass 288 A Platform

event.top_event: Crane boom structural damage and loss of load control
actions_taken: Crane placed out of service by person in charge
Crane maintenance contractor called to investigate boom winch noise
Boom hoist disassembled and planetary gears inspected
Job Safety Analysis conducted for emergency load lowering procedure
Specialized Motor Vessel with crane hired to remove damaged boom
Crane boom assembly replaced in field
Annual inspection and 100% pull test performed per API RP 2C
Crane returned to full service after post-test inspection
event.summary: On 15 October 2019 at 0755 hours, a crane boom dropped from a 45-degree angle onto the boom rest at Talos Petroleum's Main Pass 288 A platform during unscheduled maintenance. The boom was being lowered using an unapproved emergency load lowering procedure with an improper brake release tool (a 1/4" threaded bolt instead of the required fixed-head nut tool). The spiral lock ring in the brake actuator failed, causing loss of load control and the boom to fall approximately 30 feet, resulting in severe structural damage to the outermost 20-foot boom mid-section. No injuries or damage to process equipment or platform structure occurred. Root causes included inadequate communication between operator and contractor, lack of proper documentation and approval for the procedure used, and mechanical failures in the brake assembly.

threats.name: Brake system failure during load lowering
Loss of load control due to spiral lock ring failure
Use of unapproved emergency load lowering procedure
Use of incorrect brake release tool
Inadequate communication between contractor and operator

consequences.name: Impact damage to boom rest structure

controls.name: Brake band mechanical load holding system
controls.lod_basis: Direct mechanical load holding on drum

supporting_text: The brake band had the ability to withhold the load of the boom
The spiral lock ring failed. At this point, the mechanical brake had no load holding capability to stop the boom from falling
The Lead Mechanic quickly tried to stop the boom from falling by turning the bolt counterclockwise. The boom was descending too fast for the mechanic to keep up

controls.name: Counterbalance valve for controlled lowering
controls.lod_basis: Restricts fluid flow from hydraulic cylinder

supporting_text: A counterbalance valve restricts fluid flow from the hydraulic cylinder to promote controlled lowering. However, the failed gearbox rendered the counterbalance valve inoperable

controls.name: Proper brake release tool specification and use
controls.barrier_role: Ensure correct operation of brake actuator
controls.lod_basis: Procedural requirement for emergency load lowering

supporting_text: After reviewing the Seatrax emergency load lowering procedure, BSEE determined that the brake release tool required a fixed head nut, adjustment nut and flat washer. However, the tool that was used was not fixed to the bolt
a 1/4" all thread bolt was utilized in the brake actuator
This bolt is commonly used as a fastener on process vessels and pipe flanges
it is possible when the boom lowered, the nut was turning and not the bolt itself

controls.name: Job Safety Analysis (JSA) documentation and approval
controls.barrier_role: Ensure work procedures are documented and approved
controls.lod_basis: Procedural control for work planning and approval

supporting_text: The Talos and Seatrax's JSAs for the crane work performed on 15 October 2019 did not mention utilizing the emergency load lowering procedure, and the procedure was not approved by Seatrax or Talos
The JSA was written for lowering the boom and removing the boom winch drum and shaft
the Seatrax employees deviated from the work scope and did not communicate this to their supervisor

controls.name: Supervisor approval and communication of work scope changes
controls.barrier_role: Ensure work scope changes are communicated and approved

supporting_text: Per the Seatrax Account Manager, there was no conversation, or approval given to use the emergency load lowering procedure
the Seatrax employees deviated from the work scope and did not communicate this to their supervisor
the Seatrax Account Manager received a phone call informing him that the mechanics had made an attempt to lower the boom using the emergency load lowering procedure

controls.name: Gearbox and thrust bearing maintenance and inspection
controls.barrier_role: Prevent mechanical failure of load holding systems
controls.lod_basis: Preventive maintenance to avoid equipment degradation

supporting_text: Wear and tear caused a failure of a thrust bearing which made the crane inoperable
Seatrax determined the thrust bearing was the initial failure point. Debris from the failed thrust bearing traveled into the gears causing damage to the planetary gear teeth
The mechanics took the hoist apart and noticed the planetary gears were heavily damaged

controls.name: Emergency load lowering procedure approval and authorization
controls.barrier_role: Restrict use of emergency procedures to actual emergencies
controls.lod_basis: Management control to prevent misuse of emergency procedures

supporting_text: Seatrax and Talos management would only approve of this method in an emergency situation
According to the Seatrax Account Manager, the emergency load lowering procedure should not have been performed because it was not an emergency
The mechanics took it upon themselves to use the emergency load lowering procedure to save time
API RP 2C section 13.8 'Personnel Emergency Load Lowering' illustrates when to utilize the procedure

controls.name: Barricading and area isolation of crane work zone
controls.barrier_role: Prevent personnel exposure to falling load hazard
controls.lod_basis: Post-incident control to prevent secondary injuries

supporting_text: The crane boom was properly secured and the area around the crane was barricaded

controls.name: Post-incident inspection and testing per API RP 2C
controls.barrier_role: Verify equipment integrity before return to service

supporting_text: The Seatrax mechanics performed an annual inspection, tested the hoists and brakes and performed a 100% pull test on the repaired crane per the requirements of American Petroleum Institute Recommended Practice 2C (API RP 2C)
The crane was placed back into full service upon completion of the post test inspection

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
